raw_segments: The video opens with a sepia-toned, textured background featuring symmetrical, ink-blot-like patterns, setting a somber, historical tone. The title 'THE WHITE MAN'S PLAGUE' appears in bold, red serif font against a dark, grungy backdrop, accompanied by eerie, low, sustained string music. The scene transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, who stands behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom setting, speaking directly to the camera with a serious, authoritative expression. He begins, "Here is where the romanticization of tuberculosis becomes, believe it or not, even more insidious," explaining that consumption was seen as "a disease of civilization." Behind him, a chalkboard displays medical notes from 1909, reinforcing the historical and academic context. The video then cuts to a split-screen montage of black-and-white archival footage, illustrating the historical context: a classroom scene, a nurse with patients in a hospital, a scientist working with test tubes, and a close-up of a handwritten hospital record. The Man in Blue Suit continues, detailing how "city dwellers were far more likely to develop consumption" and how this led to a "racialization of the illness." He reappears, now with a text overlay quoting 'a disease of the master race not of the slave race,' visually emphasizing the racial undertones of the historical medical narrative. He elaborates that "most white doctors believed that TB could only infect white people," and it was "sometimes known as 'the white man's plague.'" The sequence concludes with the Man in Blue Suit continuing his lecture, framed by the vintage classroom environment, as the text overlay remains on screen, underscoring the video’s critical examination of historical medical racism, all set against the persistent, somber background music.

The segment opens with dark, abstract, ink-blot-like patterns evolving into fractal designs, over which elegant serif text appears, stating "a disease of the master race not of the slave race," a phrase immediately echoed by the male narrator. He continues, quoting Frank Snowden, "In the United States, the prevailing wisdom was that African Americans contracted a different disease," as the background shifts to a black-and-white historical world map with radiating lines. The visuals then show a hand pulling a file from a cabinet labeled with colonial territories like 'CAYMAN ISLANDS' and 'KENYA,' while the narrator explains how this phenomenon extended to other colonial empires where many believed TB was either rare or non-existent among people of color. Archival footage of colonial-era people, split-screen shots of maps of India and South Africa alongside crowds and individuals, and scenes of people lining up for medical examination illustrate the historical context. The narrator emphasizes that in reality, there was extensive illness and death from consumption in colonial South Asia and Africa, but it went largely undetected by colonial authorities because the premise of colonialism relied on white supremacy and the idea that only "supreme people" could contract such diseases. The scene transitions to a modern setting where the narrator, a man in a blue suit and glasses, stands behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom, gesturing expressively as he lectures, with a chalkboard behind him displaying 1909 medical notes on 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities.' He concludes by stating that acknowledging consumption was common among enslaved, colonized, and marginalized people would have undermined not just the theory of consumption, but the broader colonial premise.

The video opens with the Man in Blue Suit, a male lecturer with a clear, articulate voice, standing behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom setting. He delivers a lecture, gesturing expressively, discussing the 'aspect of colonialism itself' and how, toward the end of the 19th century, 'the romanticization of consumption began to decline' as illness was no longer seen as exclusively a disease of the 'rich and brilliant.' He explains that scholars shifted their focus from 'languorous, fainting young women and their romantic lovers' to 'the miserable humanity living in the dreary tenements born of the industrial revolution.' As he speaks of 'tentacular cities' where 'men, women, and children, pale too, often cold and starving, working long hours in dark and crowded shops, breathing smoke and coal dust,' the scene cuts to a series of grainy, blue-tinted archival clips depicting intimate embraces, bustling street scenes, city crowds, industrial workers around machinery, and a crowded room with people at desks, visually illustrating the harsh realities of early 20th-century urban and industrial life. The Man in Blue Suit then states that 'Tuberculosis was there, breeding suffering and misery, without romance.' The video returns to him briefly before transitioning to a triptych of medical imagery, featuring sepia-toned portraits of a bearded man (likely Robert Koch) flanking a central microscopic view of rod-shaped bacteria labeled 'm. tuberculosis' with the year '1882,' emphasizing the historical discovery of the tuberculosis bacterium. He concludes by noting that 'arguments raged over what caused the disease.'

The video opens with a triptych of sepia-toned portraits of Robert Koch flanking a central panel displaying 'm. tuberculosis' over a microscopic image of rod-shaped bacteria, with the text 'discovered by Robert Koch' and a lower panel showing magnified bacteria. A male voice, later identified as the Man in Blue Suit, narrates in an academic tone, stating, "1882, when a vexatious little organism called M. tuberculosis was first identified by the physician Robert Koch." The scene transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, a man with short, graying hair, wearing a blue suit, white shirt, and blue-rimmed glasses, standing behind a wooden desk in a vintage classroom setting. A chalkboard behind him displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs.' He gestures expressively as he lectures, explaining how "the era of consumption as an inherited condition... slowly faded away," and that "Now, it was a germ disease, an illness of filth and overcrowding and poverty." As he concludes, stating "The era of tuberculosis had begun," the video cuts to a grainy, blue-tinted archival montage showing cityscapes, children, industrial workers, and crowded classrooms, evoking early 20th-century urban life. A vintage-sounding male voice then announces, "from your local Tuberculosis Association," accompanied by dramatic, orchestral music with a vintage feel. This is followed by a close-up of the Man in Blue Suit, then a black-and-white shot of pamphlets titled 'HOME CARE OF TUBERCULOSIS,' with subtitles like 'A Guide for the Family' and 'Pointers for Nurses,' highlighting historical public health efforts. The Man in Blue Suit reappears, continuing his lecture, before the video cuts to a black-and-white archival shot of a city street with a horse-drawn carriage, reinforcing the historical context. The sequence ends with a bold red text overlay on a dark background: 'MAN GOT TO TELL HIMSELF HE UNDERSTAND,' as the Man in Blue Suit's academic narration resumes, explaining, "Today, we understand the explosion of TB in the 19th century to have been primarily a result of the Industrial Revolution, which led to cramped..."

The video opens with a montage of black-and-white archival footage depicting early 20th-century urban and rural life, including industrial workers and crowded scenes, as a male voice begins to explain how 'living conditions in cities where workers could easily spread TB to one another' and 'widespread malnutrition' made people 'especially vulnerable.' The speaker, identified as the Man in Blue Suit, continues his lecture in a vintage classroom setting, standing behind a wooden desk with a chalkboard displaying medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions.' He states that 'just as Britain was ground zero for the industrial revolution, it was ground zero for the rise of tuberculosis,' and that 'TB grow with industrialization in other communities as well, from India to Nigeria.' The visual then shifts to a rural wooden house where a woman interviews seated individuals on a porch, including a woman with a dog, followed by a grainy, blue-tinted archival shot of a man on a porch with a 'Tuberculosis Prevention' poster in the background. The speaker elaborates on 'TB's parallel journey with capital,' having been 'seen in outbreak after outbreak,' noting that 'industrialization wasn't seen as the culprit' at the time. He explains that as understandings of TB shifted, 'racialized stigma did a 180,' moving from black and brown people being 'incapable of getting such a beautiful disease' to 'now, they were blamed for it.' The final frames show a man conversing with seated individuals on a porch, accompanied by a text overlay quoting Dr. William C. Woodward: 'The negro race suffers from tuberculosis, tainting the country,' which the speaker directly quotes, reinforcing the disturbing racialized medical narratives.

The video opens with the Man in Blue Suit, speaking with a clear, authoritative voice, lecturing from behind a wooden desk in a dimly lit vintage classroom. A chalkboard behind him displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Toxic Insanities' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions.' As the camera zooms in on his serious expression and blue-rimmed glasses, he explains how "racialized medicine" initially linked high consumption rates among white people to white superiority, then shifted to attribute high consumption rates among Black people to the same concept. He emphatically states, "none of that was true," asserting that Black people's susceptibility to tuberculosis was "because of racism," not inherent racial factors. This point is underscored by a black-and-white historical photograph of a man writing at a desk, followed by a Text Overlay quoting, 'the negro is to blame for his own susceptibility to tuberculosis,' attributed to W. Michael Byrd and Linda A. Clayton. A split-screen then appears, showing a classroom on the left and a nurse with patients in a hospital on the right, as he elaborates that TB "especially preys on those who are marginalized... because they are marginalized." He highlights that this "racialized medicine was challenged," calling it "obvious hogwash from the beginning," and noting "loud rejection, especially from Black healthcare workers." The sequence culminates with a critical Text Overlay, 'smacked more of the cheap politician seeking notoriety and office by playing to passion and prejudice than a doctor discussing, philosophically, a scientific subject for the diffusion of knowledge,' from the same book, overlaid on archival images of a lab with test tubes and people boarding a mobile medical unit, as the lecturer recounts a Black physician's powerful response to such racist medical claims.

The video opens with a text overlay quoting W. Michael Byrd and Linda A. Clayton, criticizing a 'cheap politician' for exploiting passion and prejudice rather than discussing science philosophically, a quote read aloud by the Man in Blue Suit. This text is displayed over two archival black-and-white images: one of a lab with test tubes and another of people boarding a mobile medical unit. The scene transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, the primary speaker, standing in a vintage classroom with a chalkboard behind him listing medical topics from 1909, including 'Toxic Insanities' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions.' He speaks expressively, gesturing with his hands, explaining that bias against marginalized people and healthcare workers has proven to be one of the great facilitators of tuberculosis over the last century. The video then cuts to a colorized historical photograph of a family eating together, followed by a black-and-white close-up of a woman in a patterned dress, accompanied by a low, atmospheric, and slightly eerie musical score. The Man in Blue Suit reappears, now in a vintage laboratory filled with bottles and equipment, continuing his lecture, detailing how even after establishing tuberculosis as an infectious disease caused by a bacterium, nobody was quite sure how it spread. A title card reading 'TREATMENTS AND THE CURE' appears over an abstract, dark, kaleidoscopic background. The Man in Blue Suit continues speaking in the lab, then the video cuts to grainy black-and-white footage of a bubbling chemical reaction under a microscope, as he states, 'man got to tell himself he understand.' A text overlay appears, echoing his words: 'man got to tell himself he understand.' The scene shifts to a blue-tinted archival shot of a crowded industrial or hospital setting, followed by a black-and-white title card reading 'TUBERCULOSIS ASSOCIATION Presents,' while the speaker discusses how attention focused on places and environments that seemed to foster outbreaks, such as crowded housing tenements and dirty factories. He concludes by mentioning public health efforts like covering one's cough or sneeze with a handkerchief, as the final shot shows a nurse in a white uniform and cap, wiping her face with a cloth, with a red circle highlighting her hand and the cloth, suggesting a moment of emotional or physical strain.

The segment opens with a close-up of a man's face, a red circle highlighting his beard, as a male voice begins to explain how certain practices 'did lessen the risk of spreading TB,' including 'discouraging spitting in public places like trolley cars and sidewalks,' which was common in the US at the time. The Man in Blue Suit, standing in a vintage laboratory, speaks directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, continuing to narrate how 'people were also told not to kiss babies, or kiss at all.' He adds that 'another obsession was with dirt and dust, which again changed fashion, grooming and social habits.' The scene then transitions to a split-screen of black-and-white archival footage, showing a classroom and a hospital setting, as the narrator quotes, 'There is no way of computing the number of bacteria and noxious germs that may lurk in the Amazonian jungles of a well-whiskered face.' This is followed by a sepia-toned portrait of Dr. Edwin Bowers, with the quote continuing, 'but their numbers must be legion,' attributed to him in 1916. The video returns to the Man in Blue Suit, now with a text overlay reading 'the revolt against the whisker,' as he explains how 'fear of TB germs getting caught in beards led to what Harper's Weekly called the revolt against the whisker, ushering in an era of clean shaves.' The visual shifts to grainy, blue-tinted archival footage depicting early 20th-century urban life, showing people in period attire and a man polishing a woman's shoe, while the narrator adds, 'For women, hemlines grew shorter as anxiety rose that floor-length dresses might pick up TB germs off dirty floors.' The sequence concludes with a close-up of a hand polishing a black shoe, as the narrator mentions, 'Vidyu Krishnan points out that as women's hemlines rose a few inches at the beginning of the 1900s…'

The video opens with grainy, blue-tinted archival footage of a man polishing a woman’s shoe, as a male voice states that "shoes became an important feature of women's fashion." This transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, who narrates directly to the camera from a vintage laboratory, explaining that "By the early 20th century, entire industries had grown up in the US to try to treat tuberculosis." The scene cuts to black-and-white archival footage of a man writing at a desk, followed by a patient in a hospital bed with the bold white text 'YOU CAN LICK TB' superimposed, all set to a bluesy song about "the TB blues." The footage then shows a sprawling rural landscape with a large institutional building, likely a sanatorium, as the narrator explains that "Dry air, sunshine, and rest were believed to be the best available treatments, and so sanatoriums where consumptives could rest and breathe fresh air popped up around the world." A sepia-toned map of the United States appears with the text 'THE LAND OF NEW LUNGS' and a red circle highlighting the West Coast, transitioning to a network of roads, while the narrator details how "Southern California came to be known as 'The Land of New Lungs,' and in a mass migration that rivaled the Gold Rush, consumptive patients traveled west." This is followed by a vintage car driving through a wooded area, then a group of men in hats eating from metal bowls. The final frames show a split-screen: on the left, a hospital ward with patients in beds and a nurse; on the right, the exterior of a large institutional building, as the narrator concludes that "By 1920, around 10% of all people living in New Mexico were TB..." before the audio cuts off.

The video opens with a split-screen of black-and-white historical photographs, showing children in white smocks in a garden with a nurse, and patients in winter attire under a tree, as the Man in Blue Suit narrates about patients living in sanatoriums where dry air and open spaces were believed to heal lungs. The scene transitions to a black-and-white archival clip titled “You Can Lick TB” from 1949, featuring a man playing an upbeat, vintage-style folk/country song with male vocals, followed by a nurse pushing a cart down a sterile corridor. The footage then shows a man in a robe by a window and a frail young man in a hospital bed, as the Man in Blue Suit continues, explaining that life in these sanatoriums was "excruciatingly dull" and the job of the "invalid" was to "lie very still." A split-screen follows, showing a dimly lit hospital room on the left and a scenic mountain view on the right, accompanied by the Man in Blue Suit quoting Elizabeth Mooney: "I do nothing all day except lie here staring at the mountains. I wish they would rearrange them a bit." This is followed by a black-and-white photograph of a "TUBERCULOSIS CAMP, ILLS" in the snow, depicting a man outside a tent. The segment concludes with the Man in Blue Suit, standing in a vintage laboratory surrounded by scientific equipment, gesturing as he speaks directly to the camera, explaining that family visits were discouraged not only due to infection risk but also because patients "had to rest" and "couldn't be excited."

The minute opens with the Man in Blue Suit, in a vintage laboratory filled with historical medical paraphernalia, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. He explains that patients were often "discouraged even from reading books" and that "there was no YouTube." While his voice continues, the video transitions to a series of black-and-white archival footage clips: a large institutional building, likely a sanatorium, in a rural landscape; a man reading a newspaper in a hospital bed with a nurse; a bustling city street with a horse-drawn carriage; a snowy rural road with a vintage car; and a group of men eating from metal bowls in a communal setting. His narration discusses how "many patients did recover in sanatoriums," with "rest and adequate nutrition" being beneficial, but notes that "recovery rates don't seem to have been much higher for those taking the so-called traveling cure than for those who lived at home as invalids." The video then cuts back to the Man in Blue Suit, who continues his lecture, explaining that "separating millions of the ill from their homes did decrease the spread of the disease within families" and that combined with "better overall nutrition, safer housing, and lower rates of poverty, tuberculosis declined around the world." A sepia-toned animated world map then appears, showing glowing red veins spreading across continents, symbolizing the global reach of tuberculosis. Text overlays display '1882 - 1930 mortality from TB in the US' and '80%', as the Man in Blue Suit's voiceover confirms that "Between 1882 and 1930, overall mortality from the disease in the US fell by around 80%." The Man in Blue Suit reappears, continuing his explanation that "those improvements were not evenly distributed in the American population." He elaborates that "The declines for African Americans and Chinese Americans were much lower, and for indigenous people, there was very little decline at all." The segment concludes with a triptych of black-and-white animated medical imagery, showing a stylized cityscape with glowing medical symbols, a boy drawing a large tube, and a silhouette of a person holding a pen, suggesting themes of medical discovery and education.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a medical and biological theme, highlighting the historical struggle against tuberculosis despite advancements in diagnostic tools, as explained by the Man in Blue Suit in a vintage laboratory setting, detailing the discovery of drugs to combat M. tuberculosis and the challenges posed by the bacteria's waxy coating and subsequent drug resistance. Archival footage illustrates improved TB treatment and case-finding, but the narrator notes the continued high death rates in impoverished communities and inconsistent treatment in colonized regions, leading to a disparity in access to curative therapy, a point emphasized by Dr. Peter Mugyenyi's quote about the unequal distribution of drugs. The Man in Blue Suit elaborates on the lack of new TB drugs developed between 1965 and 2012 due to declining profit incentives, contrasting manual and automated pharmaceutical processes in a split-screen montage. He asserts that a system driven solely by profit is not the only way to develop drugs, highlighting the disproportionate reward for developing drugs for cosmetic purposes over life-saving TB treatments, illustrated by a sepia-toned animated world map showing TB mortality rates. Archival footage shows a scientist using a microscope and a printing press producing public health leaflets, as the Man in Blue Suit describes the rise in TB deaths associated with the HIV/AIDS pandemic. He recounts meeting Phumeza Tisile, a young South African woman diagnosed with TB as a teenager, whose initial symptoms were misdiagnosed, leading to severe breathing difficulties and the eventual discovery of extensive tuberculosis in her lungs. The Man in Blue Suit explains the Directly Observed Therapy Short-Course (DOTS) protocol, designed to ensure medication compliance due to a lack of trust in patients, but also acknowledges the challenges it poses for patients requiring daily medication and the complexities of patient compliance, as illustrated by hospital scenes and a quote from Dr. Jennifer Furin. Phumeza's condition worsened, leading to her hospitalization and diagnosis of multi-drug resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB), causing her immense fear, as she researched the disease online and saw images of people with exposed ribs. The Man in Blue Suit details her painful treatment regimen, including injections that caused permanent hearing loss, emphasizing that earlier molecular testing could have prevented her suffering. Despite taking 30,000 pills, she was eventually cured, a reality that persists for many patients in 2024, even with new, shorter treatment protocols. The Man in Blue Suit highlights Fumeza's activism, showing her speaking at a protest, presenting a Drug Resistant TB Manifesto at the United Nations, and advocating for access to life-saving drugs like Bedaquiline. He details the lawsuit filed by Fumeza and Nandita Venkatesan, supported by Doctors Without Borders, against Johnson & Johnson, which successfully prevented the extension of a drug patent, making bedaquiline more affordable and accessible, enabling treatment for over 50,000 people with MDR-TB annually. The Man in Blue Suit emphasizes that until recently, the WHO's recommendations for drug-resistant TB were essentially supportive care, as he contrasts this with the success of Partners in Health in achieving similar cure rates in poor communities as in wealthy hospitals. He criticizes the high prices of effective TB drugs due to pharmaceutical companies, noting that making these drugs less rare could lead to further antibiotic resistance. The Man in Blue Suit passionately argues against reserving powerful TB drugs for the wealthy, questioning how he could deny the best available treatment to his own child or to Fumeza's family, and asks how many of the 1.5 million people expected to die of TB this year would survive if they had access to the same healthcare as the wealthy in developed countries.

Triptych of Medical Imagery.description: A three-panel display showing a stylized cityscape with glowing medical symbols, a boy drawing a large tube, and a silhouette of a person holding a pen, possibly drawing an X-ray, suggesting themes of medical discovery and education.

Tubercle Animation.description: A stylized, sepia-toned animation showing a rod-shaped bacterium being enveloped by translucent circular structures that multiply and form a flower-like pattern, with the word 'Tubercle' appearing in bold white text above the central formation, illustrating the biological process of tuberculosis formation.

Man in Blue Suit.description: A man with short, graying hair, wearing a blue suit, white shirt, and blue-rimmed glasses, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, standing at a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom with a chalkboard displaying medical notes from 1909. He speaks with a clear, earnest, and passionate voice, discussing antibiotic resistance, the ethics of healthcare access, and TB mortality, referencing 'Fumeza's family' and 'tuberculosis doctor, K.J. Sung'.

Animated Anatomical Sequence.description: A stylized animation depicting a human figure with visible internal organs, particularly the lungs, and a dark, spreading infection in the chest, transitioning to a patient in bed with medical supplies, illustrating the progression of disease.

Archival Footage (Rural Road).description: Black-and-white archival footage of a snowy rural road with a vintage car driving through a wooded area, symbolizing travel and movement.

Archival Footage (Shoe Polishing).description: Grainy, blue-tinted archival footage showing a man in a hat and coat polishing a woman’s shoe in a public setting, evoking early 20th-century urban life and social interactions.

Archival Footage (Hospital Patient).description: Black-and-white archival footage showing a man in a hospital bed, with a nurse attending to him, and the bold white text 'YOU CAN LICK TB' superimposed, directly illustrating the public health campaigns and treatment efforts against tuberculosis mentioned by the narrator.

Archival Footage (Men Eating).description: Black-and-white archival footage showing a group of men in hats eating from metal bowls, possibly patients or workers, in a communal setting.

Archival Footage (City Street Scene).description: A black-and-white archival shot of a city street with a horse-drawn carriage, buildings, and pedestrians, reinforcing the early 20th-century historical context.

Archival Footage (Institutional Building.description: Black-and-white archival footage showing a large, multi-story institutional building, likely a sanatorium, set in a rural landscape with bare trees, visually emphasizing the scale of tuberculosis treatment facilities discussed by the narrator.

Dr. Peter Mugyenyi.description: A man in a dark suit with arms crossed, wearing glasses and a patterned tie, set against a sepia-toned backdrop with vaccine vials visible on either side. He is identified by the narrator as 'the Ugandan physician Dr. Peter Mugyenyi,' whose powerful quote about healthcare disparity – 'Where are the drugs? The drugs are where the disease is not. And where is the disease? The disease is where the drugs are not.' – is both spoken by the narrator and displayed as a text overlay beside him.

Archival Lab Footage.description: Black-and-white historical footage showing a scientist in a lab, intently working with a microscope and glassware, visually representing the early medical research and diagnostic techniques for TB as described by the narrator.

Split-Screen Pill Production.description: A split-screen showing a scientist in a lab coat handling test tubes on the left and a conveyor belt of white pills on the right, emphasizing modern pharmaceutical manufacturing, appearing as the speaker continues his critique of profit-driven drug development.

Text Overlays.description: Elegant serif text appearing within dark, circular ink-like frames, displaying the phrase 'antibiotic resistance' in white, suggesting a medical or public health initiative.

Split-Screen Modern Pharmaceutical Produ.description: A split-screen showing a woman in a blue uniform at a desk on the left, and an automated machine filling amber bottles with liquid on the right, highlighting the contrast between manual and industrial pharmaceutical processes. Overlaid text quotes Dr. Carole Mitnick, which is spoken aloud by the Man in Blue Suit, emphasizing the 'human-manufactured problem' of disease treatment.

Split-Screen Bacteria Animation.description: A side-by-side animated comparison showing the growth of 'Mycobacterium tuberculosis' (slow, sparse multiplication) and 'E. coli' (rapid, dense multiplication) on a brown, textured background, with labels in white serif font.

Split-Screen Vintage Ads.description: A split-screen montage of vintage advertisements, including one for A.L. Moody’s Corset Dept. and another for a dandruff remedy with the slogan 'To be Pretty, A Woman’s Hair Must be free From Dandruff,' showcasing early 20th-century consumer culture and beauty standards.

Vintage Advertisement.description: Grainy, blue-tinted archival footage sequence showing a man in a hat and coat, a woman in a dress, and a man polishing a woman’s shoe in a public setting, evoking early 20th-century urban life and social interactions.

Animated World Map.description: A sepia-toned animated world map with glowing red veins spreading across continents, symbolizing the global reach of tuberculosis, with text overlays indicating '1882 - 1930 mortality from TB in the US' and '80%'. This visual accompanies the Man in Blue Suit's narration about the economic drivers of drug development and the historical impact of TB.

Text Overlays (Critical Quote).description: A bold serif text overlay reading 'smacked more of the cheap politician seeking notoriety and office by playing to passion and prejudice than a doctor discussing, philosophically, a scientific subject for the diffusion of knowledge,' attributed to W. Michael Byrd and Linda A. Clayton, displayed over two archival images—one of a lab with test tubes and another of people boarding a mobile medical unit.

Historical Protest Footage.description: Grainy, vintage footage of a protest march with demonstrators carrying a banner reading 'WE DEMAND AN END TO LESBIAN GAY OPPRESSION' and 'LESBIAN GAY TASK FORCE,' marching down a city street lined with buildings and vehicles, evoking a moment of social activism.

AIDS School Transportation Bus.description: A yellow school bus with 'AIDS SCHOOL TRANSPORTATION CO.' written on it, with a man in a suit and tie standing beside it, indicating a public health initiative related to HIV/AIDS education or outreach.

Vintage Classroom Setting.description: A dimly lit, old-fashioned classroom with a large chalkboard displaying medical notes from 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs'. The desk is wooden, and vintage medical equipment and bottles are visible on the side, contributing to the historical and academic atmosphere.

Vigil Scene.description: A solemn indoor scene of a group of people holding lit candles, standing in a room with a crucifix on the wall, suggesting a vigil or memorial service, with individuals dressed in casual and semi-formal attire.

Phumeza Tisile Photograph.description: A still photograph of a young woman in a pink shirt wearing a teal surgical mask, looking through window blinds with a pensive expression. Text overlays identify her condition as 'multidrug-resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB)', and her fears about her condition, including searching online images of severe TB cases and fearing death, are directly quoted by the narrator.

Robert Koch - 1882.description: A historical illustration of Robert Koch, a balding man with a beard, wearing a lab coat, seated at a desk with a microscope and various lab equipment, labeled 'Robert Koch - 1882'. The narrator specifically highlights Koch's diagnosis of TB in 1882 using this microscopic method.

X-ray of Chest with Text Overlay.description: A grayscale X-ray image of a human chest showing dense, cloudy areas in the lungs, overlaid with a quote attributed to Phumeza Tisile, which is read aloud by the narrator: 'I was searching stuff online, and it was really, really scary to see because on Google so many of the people on Images were already dead. Their ribs were exposed, and I thought I was gonna be like that, too. I thought I was likely going to die.' This visual reinforces the severity of her condition and her expressed fears.

Historical Artworks Montage.description: A sequence of diverse historical artworks including a medieval manuscript illustration of a man in a red hat examining a patient, surrounded by others, contributing to a theme of historical illness and mortality.

Medication and Documentation Setup.description: A close-up of a wooden desk with two white pill bottles, a green and white medication box labeled 'Mylan,' and a printed document with numerical data, suggesting a clinical or pharmaceutical setting.

Man in White Lab Coat.description: A man with a beard, wearing a white lab coat over a light-colored shirt, sitting at a wooden desk, writing in a notebook, with a printer and papers nearby, suggesting a clinical or research setting.

Split-Screen Bureaucratic Office Scene.description: A split-screen showing, on the left, a person in a brown coat walking toward a glass door in a green-walled hallway; on the right, a man in a gray jacket sitting at a desk with a laptop, writing on paper, while a group of people, including women in headscarves, stand behind him, indicating a busy administrative or medical office environment.

Philosophical Text Overlay.description: Bold red serif text on a dark, textured background reading 'MAN GOT TO TELL HIMSELF HE UNDERSTAND', appearing after the Man in Blue Suit's lecture, suggesting a reflective or educational conclusion.

United Nations Exterior.description: The exterior of the United Nations building, with flags of various nations flying, serving as a significant location where the protestor, identified as Fumeza, is seen posing with two other women, one wearing a lanyard and a 'DRUG RESISTANT TB' t-shirt, highlighting her global advocacy efforts.

Drug Resistant TB Manifesto.description: A white spiral-bound document titled 'SIGNATURES: A DRUG RESISTANT TB MANIFESTO,' held by the protestor, representing a collective effort (35,000 supporters, collected March 2013-May 2014) to advocate for change in the treatment and access to medication for drug-resistant tuberculosis, a topic frequently discussed by the narrator.

Protestor with Microphone.description: A young Black woman with braided hair, wearing a white t-shirt with red text, speaking passionately into a microphone at an outdoor protest, later seen at the United Nations holding a document titled 'SIGNATURES: A DRUG RESISTANT TB MANIFESTO' and addressing a formal audience. She has a passionate female voice, making a plea for change, and is identified by the narrator as 'Fumeza,' a college graduate, sociologist, and leading voice in the fight against tuberculosis.

Person in Red Shirt and White Apron.description: A person wearing a red long-sleeve shirt and white apron, handling medical supplies such as bottles and containers on a table, suggesting a clinical or pharmacy setting.

Young Black Woman on Bench.description: A young Black woman with braided hair, wearing a beige jacket over a graphic tee, sitting on a wooden park bench, reading a document titled 'SIGNATURES: A DRUG RESISTANT TB MANIFESTO,' visually representing the 'activism and innovation' and the impact of 'Fumeza's work' in expanding access to diagnosis and treatment for TB patients, as discussed in the accompanying audio.

MEDECINS SANS FRONTIERES Ship.description: A large white ship bearing the 'MEDECINS SANS FRONTIERES' logo, with people boarding via a ramp, visually embodying the 'Doctors Without Borders' organization whose support is highlighted in the audio for its role in advocating for accessible medical treatment.

Medical Professional in White Coat.description: A person in a white medical coat and face mask, seated at a desk with a laptop, interacting with patients in a clinical setting.

MEDECINS SANS FRONTIERES CHARTRE.description: A glass panel displaying the 'MEDECINS SANS FRONTIERES CHARTRE' with its red logo and French text, visually representing the 'Doctors Without Borders' organization mentioned in the audio as supporting the efforts against high drug prices.

Automated Pharmaceutical Production Line.description: A clean, industrial setting showing a conveyor belt with clear bottles capped with orange lids moving through an automated filling and sealing process. This imagery visually reinforces the lecturer's discussion about pharmaceutical companies, drug production, and the economic factors influencing the cost and availability of medications, particularly as the 'antibiotic resistance' text overlay appears over it, linking industrial scale to medical crisis.

City Street Night Scene.description: A bustling urban street at night with pedestrians, illuminated by neon signs and a large digital screen displaying fluctuating numbers, possibly representing stock market data or public health metrics. This modern, high-tech visual serves as a contemporary backdrop to the lecturer's discussion on global health challenges and drug economics.

Text Overlays (waste/waist).description: Two text overlays on a dark, patterned background: 'waste' and 'waist,' in elegant serif font, highlighting the linguistic evolution of the word, with the Man in Blue Suit appearing in a small inset above.

raw_segments: The segment begins with a black-and-white triptych of animated and archival medical imagery, featuring a stylized human figure with internal organs and a cityscape, a boy drawing a large tube, and a silhouette examining an X-ray of lungs, establishing a medical and biological theme. A male voice immediately begins to explain that despite advancements in diagnostic and treatment tools like the stethoscope and X-ray machine, tuberculosis remained fundamentally incurable. The scene then transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, a man with short, graying hair and blue-rimmed glasses, standing in a vintage laboratory amidst wooden shelves, glass bottles, and a microscope. He speaks directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, detailing the discovery of eight different classes of drugs between 1940 and 1965 that could kill M. tuberculosis. The video cuts to a black-and-white animated sequence, illustrating detailed human lungs and a magnified view of rod-shaped bacteria multiplying within the tissue, visually representing the disease's progression. The speaker continues, revealing the "catch": the bacteria's waxy coating made it difficult to kill, necessitating long-term drug administration which fostered resistance, and no single drug was effective alone. The scene returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who, with animated gestures, concludes his lecture by explaining that it was only in the mid-1950s that a combination treatment of three drugs was tested and approved, finally making tuberculosis curable for the first time in human history.

The video opens with a series of grainy, vintage archival clips, as a male narrator begins to speak, explaining how improved treatment, case-finding, and contact tracing led to the identification and treatment of new TB cases. These visuals depict nurses gathered around a patient in a hospital, a doctor examining a document, a bustling outdoor scene with people near a mobile medical unit, a close-up of an elderly man with a white beard, a scientist in a lab handling test tubes, a man polishing a shoe, a hospital patient with the superimposed text 'YOU CAN LICK TB', a large institutional building, men eating, a city street, and a snowy rural road. The narrator continues, detailing how, despite these advancements, TB continued to kill millions annually in impoverished communities, and attempts to deliver combination therapy to colonized regions were haphazard and inconsistent, forcing many to seek whatever antibiotics they could, regardless of effectiveness. The scene then transitions to a modern, well-lit vintage laboratory where the Man in Blue Suit, with short graying hair and glasses, stands behind a wooden desk, gesturing expressively as he speaks directly to the camera, continuing his lecture. The background is filled with scholarly items like glass bottles, a microscope, and a red-bound book. The video then cuts to a sepia-toned portrait of Dr. Peter Mugyenyi, a man in a dark suit with arms crossed, set against vaccine vials, as the narrator introduces him as the Ugandan physician who, in 2000, gave a speech about the global failure to get HIV drugs into impoverished communities. The narrator then quotes Dr. Mugyenyi, whose powerful words also appear as a text overlay beside him: 'Where are the drugs? The drugs are where the disease is not. And where is the disease? The disease is where the drugs are not.' The video returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who continues his animated lecture, emphasizing that the same disparity was true for tuberculosis, with curative therapy available everywhere by the mid-1960s.

The minute opens with a textured, sepia-toned background featuring two dark, ink-blot-like shapes resembling abstract eyes, setting a mysterious tone. A male voice states, "except for where it was most needed." This is immediately followed by a bold text overlay, 'WHERE THE DRUGS ARE NOT,' with 'NOT' highlighted in red, appearing in elegant serif font within dark, circular ink-like frames, reinforcing the provocative theme. Subtle, atmospheric background music begins and continues throughout. The scene then cuts to the Man in Blue Suit, a scholarly figure with graying hair and blue-rimmed glasses, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures from behind a wooden desk in a vintage laboratory filled with bottles, a microscope, and a red-bound book. He explains that "in the 25 years between 1940 and 1965, eight different classes of drugs were developed to treat tuberculosis," but then emphasizes that "in the 47 years between 1965 and 2012, no new drugs were synthesized to treat tuberculosis." As he elaborates on how the profit incentive for researching new drugs "went away" when TB declined in rich countries, the video shifts to a split-screen. On the left, a woman in a blue uniform sits at a desk, appearing to be on the phone or reviewing documents, while on the right, a modern automated machine fills amber bottles with liquid, contrasting manual and industrial pharmaceutical processes. This is followed by another split-screen showing a scientist in a lab coat handling test tubes on the left, and a conveyor belt of white pills on the right, emphasizing modern pharmaceutical manufacturing. The Man in Blue Suit reappears, continuing his lecture with animated gestures, asserting that a system "driven exclusively by profit incentive is, of course, not the only way to develop drugs," and referencing Dr. Carol Mitnick regarding the failure to develop drugs for illnesses "that are not seen as prof..." The warm, consistent lighting focuses on the speaker and the vintage lab, while the split-screen segments introduce a modern, clinical contrast.

The minute begins with a split-screen displaying modern pharmaceutical production, showing an automated machine filling amber bottles and a woman monitoring medical imaging screens. Overlaid text quotes Dr. Carole Mitnick, which a male voice (the Man in Blue Suit) narrates: “a human-manufactured problem that needs a human solution... If medications were a public good, the burden of disease would drive the priorities of the industry, and TB treatment would be varied and plentiful.” The scene transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, speaking expressively from a vintage laboratory, continuing his lecture on how “there is nothing inevitable about living in a world where developing drugs that lengthen eyelashes is more highly rewarded than developing drugs that treat tuberculosis.” As he speaks, a sepia-toned animated world map appears, with glowing red veins symbolizing the global spread of disease, overlaid with '1882 - 1930 mortality from TB in the US' and '80%'. The video then cuts to archival black-and-white footage of a scientist using a microscope, while the narrator explains how TB death rates remained steady in much of the developing world even as they declined in rich nations. A distinct printing press sound is heard as a close-up shows a printing press producing leaflets with bold text 'AIDS' and 'DON'T DIE'. The Man in Blue Suit reappears, continuing his lecture, describing how, beginning in the early 1980s, physicians and activists sounded the alarm about an “explosion” in severe tuberculosis deaths associated with the new pandemic of HIV/AIDS, as untreated HIV lowers resistance to infection. The video concludes with a final shot of the printing press in motion, emphasizing the dissemination of public health messages.

The video opens with the Man in Blue Suit, a man with short, graying hair and glasses, seated at a wooden desk in a vintage laboratory, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. He narrates in a serious, informative tone about how individuals are "far more likely to progress to active disease as the immune system weakens," and how "far too little was done to expand access to either TB or HIV medications" in the mid-1980s, leading to "tens of millions of deaths from the intertwining pandemics of HIV and TB." The scene transitions to grainy, vintage footage of a protest march, where demonstrators carry banners reading 'WE DEMAND AN END TO LESBIAN GAY OPPRESSION' and 'LESBIAN GAY TASK FORCE,' marching down a city street. This is followed by a solemn indoor scene of a group of people holding lit candles in a room with a crucifix, suggesting a vigil. The footage then cuts to a man in a suit and tie standing beside a yellow school bus with 'AIDS SCHOOL TRANSPORTATION CO.' written on it. The video returns to the Man in Blue Suit, now in a close-up, continuing his narration, explaining that between 1982 and 2005, "roughly as many people died of tuberculosis as died in World War I and World War II combined." A title card appears with the text 'A FUNDAMENTAL MISTRUST' in elegant serif font over a dark, textured background, accompanied by a brief, somber musical interlude. The scene shifts back to the Man in Blue Suit, now standing at a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom with a chalkboard displaying medical notes from 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs.' He continues, recounting a "TB conference not long ago" where he "met a young South African woman named Phumeza Tisile, who was diagnosed with TB as a teenager." The final shot is a black-and-white photograph of Phumeza Tisile, a young woman wearing sunglasses and a hoodie, sitting on stone steps, with her name displayed in the lower left corner, as the Man in Blue Suit adds that she "just received a full scholarship to university."

The video opens with a somber still photograph of Phumeza Tisile, a young woman in a red jacket and blue jeans against a pink wall, as a male narrator describes her initial symptoms of weight loss and shortness of breath, quoting her text overlay: “So I went to the clinic and coughed into a cup.” The scene transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, who, from a vintage classroom with a chalkboard displaying 1909 medical notes, explains with expressive gestures how TB is still commonly diagnosed by examining sputum samples under a microscope. This narration is accompanied by black-and-white archival footage of a scientist working intently with a microscope and glassware, followed by a close-up of rod-shaped bacteria and a historical illustration of Robert Koch from 1882 at his microscope, as the narrator explicitly states Koch diagnosed TB this way. The Man in Blue Suit reappears, continuing to explain that microscopy often misses cases, leading to Phumeza's catastrophic misdiagnosis. The segment concludes with an X-ray of a human chest showing dense, cloudy lungs, overlaid with Phumeza's quoted words, “I was really struggling to breathe,” as the narrator recounts her worsening condition and severe breathing difficulties.

The video opens with a grayscale X-ray of a human chest, displaying dense, cloudy areas in the lungs, overlaid with the text 'I was really struggling to breathe.' A clear, articulate male voice immediately begins, describing how it was "obvious that tuberculosis was everywhere in her lungs," leading to the patient being started on "a standard TB treatment immediately." The scene then transitions to the speaker, a Man in a Blue Suit, standing at a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom, with a chalkboard behind him displaying medical notes from 1909. He speaks expressively, explaining a "detour" to describe a protocol from the late 1970s called "Directly Observed Therapy Short-Course," which appears as a text overlay in a vintage-style font. He elaborates that this protocol, known as DOTS, "really only exists because of a lack of trust that certain patients will take their medications consistently." As he continues, a close-up of a wooden desk shows two white pill bottles, a 'Mylan' medication box, and a document with numerical data, visually reinforcing his point about medication. He explains that TB is susceptible to antibiotic resistance, worsened by "haphazard treatment," and the idea behind DOTS is to have a healthcare professional "directly observe patients taking their medicine every day." This explanation coincides with a shot of a Man in a White Lab Coat writing in a notebook. The speaker concludes by noting that while the protocol initially helped by providing "regular access to adequate supplies of appropriate antibiotics," it also required "patients to find their way to a clinic or doctor," as depicted in a final split-screen scene showing a person walking in a hallway and a busy administrative office environment.

The segment opens with a split-screen montage showcasing hospital environments: on the left, a person in an orange jumpsuit walks through a green-walled hallway, while on the right, a crowded waiting room is filled with patients and medical staff. This transitions to a hospital room where a man in an orange jumpsuit sits on 'BED 2', hugging his knees, as a nurse prepares supplies. Concurrently, a male voice begins to speak, immediately setting the tone for a lecture. The scene then cuts to the Man in Blue Suit, standing at a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom, with a chalkboard behind him displaying medical notes from 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs'. He speaks expressively, gesturing with his hands, explaining that requiring daily medication for months or inpatient stays "creates extreme challenges for many people with TB." As he speaks, a text overlay appears, quoting Dr. Jennifer Furin: 'Tuberculosis is the only disease I know where the core of therapy is based on fundamental mistrust.' He continues, discussing how "antibiotic resistance is driven by so-called patient noncompliance," which also appears as a stylized text overlay. After a brief cut to a man in a white shirt walking through a dimly lit corridor, the Man in Blue Suit returns, continuing his animated lecture. He elaborates that while many TB patients fail to complete their regimen, "the concept of compliance turns out to be really complicated when you zoom in to the level of individual patient experience." A subtle, ethereal background sound emerges as he narrates a specific case, stating, "By the time she was finally diagnosed, Fumesa could not walk to the clinic by herself. She was simply too sick. And public transport was expensive, not to mention the potential to spread the illness, did her inability..." The segment concludes with a bold red serif text overlay on a dark, textured background, reading: 'MAN GOT TO TELL HIMSELF HE UNDERSTAND', serving as a philosophical conclusion to the medical discourse.

The video opens with the Man in Blue Suit, a man with short, graying hair and glasses, speaking directly to the camera from a dimly lit vintage classroom setting. He uses expressive hand gestures as he narrates Phumeza's medical journey, explaining how she was initially able to avoid daily treatment because South Africa allowed two weeks of take-home medication. Behind him, a chalkboard displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions.' He continues, detailing how Phumeza's condition didn't improve, leading to her hospitalization and the discovery that her drugs were failing due to multi-drug resistant tuberculosis (MDR-TB). The scene then cuts to a close-up of Phumeza Tisile, a young woman in a a pink shirt and teal surgical mask, looking pensively through window blinds, with text overlays confirming her MDR-TB diagnosis. The Man in Blue Suit narrates her profound fear, quoting her directly: 'I was searching stuff online, and it was really, really scary to see because on Google so many of the people on Images were already dead. Their ribs were exposed, and I thought I was gonna be like that, too. I thought I was likely going to die.' This quote is superimposed over a chest X-ray image showing dense, cloudy areas in the lungs. The video returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who continues his lecture with animated gestures, describing Phumeza's subsequent treatment regimen involving painful injections and dozens of pills taken each day.

The segment opens with a close-up of the Man in Blue Suit, dressed in a blue suit and blue-rimmed glasses, speaking directly to the camera from behind a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom setting, where a chalkboard displays medical notes from 1909. He narrates in a calm, informative voice about injections carrying a very high risk of permanent hearing loss. He then quotes a patient, Fumeza, saying, "I just woke up, and everything was quiet," a phrase that simultaneously appears as a text overlay on a dark, textured background. The scene returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who gestures expressively, emphasizing his points, as the camera zooms in on his earnest face. He explains that Fumeza's particular strain of tuberculosis was resistant to even more drugs, making it "pre-XDR TB or pre-extensively drug-resistant tuberculosis," a term also displayed as an elegant text overlay within a dark, circular ink-like frame. He continues, detailing how the painful injections caused Fumeza permanent and total hearing loss for four years until she received a cochlear implant, and that the drug "didn't help her at all." He reflects that if Fumeza had been able to access molecular testing or if her TB had been correctly identified from the beginning, she could have been saved her hearing and so much suffering. Instead, she was in treatment for tuberculosis for three years and eight months, during which time she took between 20... The warm, dim lighting consistently enhances the historical and academic ambiance of his lecture.

The segment opens with the Man in Blue Suit, a man with short, graying hair, wearing a blue suit, white shirt, and blue-rimmed glasses, speaking directly to the camera from a wooden lectern in a dimly lit vintage classroom. Behind him, a chalkboard displays medical notes from 1909, including 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions.' He speaks with a clear, articulate male voice, continuing a narrative about a patient's arduous treatment involving "30,000 pills" before being cured. He explains that this difficult reality persists for many patients in 2024, despite new treatment protocols that can cure drug-resistant TB in "6 to 9 months with 5 to 7 pills per day." He notes that these improved treatments are only "beginning to reach those who need them most," but access is increasing, "albeit belatedly." The scene then transitions to a dynamic split-screen montage, illustrating the impact of this struggle. On the left, a young Black woman, identified by the narrator as "Fumeza," speaks passionately into a microphone at an outdoor protest, while on the right, a crowd holds a banner reading 'PEOPLE OVER PROFIT #PHARMAGATE.' The montage continues, showing Fumeza at the United Nations, posing with others, and later holding a white spiral-bound document titled 'SIGNATURES: A DRUG RESISTANT TB MANIFESTO,' which she presents to a man in a suit. Her passionate female voice is heard, echoing the on-screen subtitles as she pleads, "I ask you to act now. I ask you for change. Thank you." The video cuts back to the Man in Blue Suit in the vintage classroom, who resumes his lecture, gesturing expressively. He continues to speak, introducing "Fumeza" as a "college graduate, a sociologist, and a leading voice in the fight against tuberculosis," whose efforts have increased access to life-saving drugs. He then discusses "Bedaquiline," a "critical medication for treating drug-resistant tuberculosis," first released in rich countries in 2013.

The minute opens with the Man in Blue Suit lecturing from a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom, explaining how drug prices were artificially high due to a lack of competition. He details a lawsuit filed by Fumeza and Nandita Venkatesan, with support from the organization 'Doctors Without Borders' (visually represented by a glass panel displaying the 'MEDECINS SANS FRONTIERES CHARTRE'), against Johnson & Johnson to prevent the extension of a drug patent past its 2023 expiration date. As he speaks, a person in a red shirt and white apron organizes medical supplies, and the Man in Blue Suit continues, announcing the success of the lawsuit, which allowed for generic competition and made the critical drug 'bedaquiline' far less expensive. The scene transitions to a large white 'MEDECINS SANS FRONTIERES' ship where people are boarding via a ramp, and a medical professional in a white coat and mask is seen interacting with patients, illustrating the humanitarian efforts. The lecturer emphasizes that this outcome will enable over 50,000 people with MDR-TB to receive treatment annually who otherwise would have had little chance of being cured. The segment concludes with the Man in Blue Suit highlighting Fumeza's work in expanding access to diagnosis and treatment, and her own survival due to 'activism and innovation,' as a young Black woman is shown sitting on a park bench, reading a 'SIGNATURES: A DRUG RESISTANT TB MANIFESTO' document, visually connecting to the public health advocacy discussed.

The segment opens with the Man in Blue Suit, a man with short, graying hair and blue-rimmed glasses, delivering a lecture with expressive hand gestures from a wooden lectern in a dimly lit Vintage Classroom Setting. Behind him, a chalkboard displays historical medical notes from January 26, 1909, detailing 'Toxic Insanities' and 'Pathology of Gross Lesions.' The Man in Blue Suit's clear, articulate voice explains that until quite recently, the World Health Organization's recommendations for drug-resistant TB were "supportive care," which Dr. Carol Mitnick summarized as "put people in a hut by the side of the road and wait for them to die." This exact quote appears as a Text Overlay on screen. The scene transitions to a split-screen: the Man in Blue Suit continues speaking in the top right, while the top left shows a bustling City Street Night Scene with a large digital screen displaying fluctuating numbers. The bottom panel features an Automated Pharmaceutical Production Line with clear bottles moving along a conveyor belt. He continues, detailing how in the late 1990s, Partners in Health proved that similar cure rates for MDRTB could be achieved in poor communities as in the world's finest hospitals. The video cuts back to a full-screen view of the Man in Blue Suit, who emphasizes that many effective drugs for highly resistant tuberculosis remain "very expensive," not due to their components, but because "prices are kept artificially high by pharmaceutical companies." As the split-screen returns, the Text Overlay 'antibiotic resistance' fades in over the pharmaceutical production line, aligning with his concluding point that making these drugs less rare could lead to "further antibiotic resistance." The overall atmosphere is academic and urgent, blending historical context with modern public health concerns, driven by the speaker's compelling narrative.

The video opens with a close-up of the Man in Blue Suit, a man with short, graying hair, wearing a blue suit, white shirt, and blue-rimmed glasses, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures. He stands at a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom setting, where a chalkboard behind him displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, covering 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs,' with illustrative cases like Anemia, Hemorrhage, Brain Tumor, and Syphilis, alongside the names 'Professor Sterne' and 'Dr. Jackson.' The warm, academic lighting highlights the historical atmosphere. The man's voice is clear and earnest as he discusses the fear of antibiotic resistance, emphasizing that the terrifying prospect of a world unable to treat bacterial infections shouldn't lead to reserving powerful and efficacious drugs for the rich. He passionately states he would not accept his own child being denied the best available treatment for TB, questioning how he could ask 'Fumeza's family' to accept such a world. As he speaks, the camera alternates between close-ups of his face and wider shots. A gentle, slightly melancholic musical interlude with a subtle chime sound accompanies a visual transition to a dark, textured background displaying the bold text 'THE WORLD WE CHOOSE' in gold and red. The scene then returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who continues his lecture with animated gestures. He recounts asking a tuberculosis doctor, K.J. Sung, about the 1.5 million people expected to die of TB this year, and how many would survive if they had access to the kind of healthcare afforded to the wealthy in the UK, Japan, or the US. He concludes by noting that while TB is often curable, it remains a difficult disease to treat, especially in cases of extensive drug resistance. The segment ends with a beige, patterned card posing the critical public health inquiry: 'How many would die if everyone could access good healthcare?' in elegant serif font, framed by a subtle floral design.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with text questioning the mortality rate if good healthcare were universally accessible, immediately answered by Dr. KJ Seung stating, 'None. Zero. Zero people should die of TB.' The Man in Blue Suit, delivering a lecture from a vintage classroom with a 1909 chalkboard, passionately asserts that a world without TB deaths is achievable through 'long-term, large-scale investments by rich countries to strengthen the healthcare systems of impoverished countries,' illustrated by visuals of Sierra Leone and a rural street scene. He highlights the need for 'training and employment of far more healthcare workers and likely investments in new treatments,' shown through images of community engagement and the Partners In Health team. The speaker concludes that 21st-century TB is 'not really caused by a bacteria that we know how to kill,' and the segment briefly shows a historical photograph of Dr. A. Wilberforce Williams. The narrative then shifts to a split-screen, contrasting Dr. A. Wilberforce Williams with a quote listing 'poverty, bad housing, bad sanitation, long hours, high rent, [and] poor food' as historical causes of TB. The Man in Blue Suit elaborates that in the 21st century, 'us' is the cause, presenting both bad and good news: while in 1800 nothing could be done, now, 'if we are the cause of TB, we can also be the solution.' This is underscored by a montage of historical artworks and photographs depicting the disease's historical impact. Finally, a bar graph illustrates a decline in TB deaths from 2.3 million in 2000 to 1.5 million in 2021, with the speaker noting the potential for further decline by 2032.

raw_segments: The minute opens with text overlays on a vintage-style beige card, posing the question, 'How many would die if everyone could access good healthcare?' A male voice, identified as Dr. KJ Seung, immediately answers with a bold 'None. Zero. Zero people should die of TB.' The scene transitions to a close-up of the Man in Blue Suit, who is the speaker, delivering a passionate lecture directly to the camera with expressive gestures from a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom, complete with a chalkboard displaying medical notes from 1909. He emphasizes that 'we could choose to live in a world where no one dies of TB,' acknowledging that this choice would require 'long-term, large-scale investments by rich countries to strengthen the healthcare systems of impoverished countries.' As he speaks, the visuals shift to a sepia-toned map highlighting Sierra Leone, followed by a street scene in a rural area with people and a white delivery truck, illustrating the context of impoverished regions. The camera then moves into a building marked 'OLD TB WARD,' revealing a computer monitor displaying a chest X-ray with visible lung damage, underscoring the disease's impact. The speaker continues, detailing the need for 'training and employment of far more healthcare workers and likely investments in new treatments.' The visuals show him sitting under a tree with local community members, followed by a group photo of individuals wearing orange 'Partners In Health' shirts, symbolizing collaborative efforts. Returning to his lecture, the Man in Blue Suit concludes that TB in the 21st century 'is not really caused by a bacteria that we know how to kill.' The video then displays a black-and-white historical photograph of a man with round glasses and a mustache, whom the speaker identifies as 'Dr. A. Wilberforce Williams,' before fading to black.

The segment opens with a split-screen, displaying a black-and-white historical photograph of Dr. A. Wilberforce Williams, a bald man with round glasses and a mustache, on the left. On the right, a quote in elegant serif font lists 'poverty, bad housing, bad sanitation, bad working conditions, long hours, high rent, [and] poor food.' A male voice, belonging to the Man in Blue Suit, immediately begins speaking, stating that 'a century ago, the real causes of TB are poverty, bad housing, bad sanitation, bad working conditions, long hours, high rent, and poor food,' directly referencing the on-screen text. The scene then transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, with short, graying hair and blue-rimmed glasses, standing at a wooden lectern in a dimly lit vintage classroom. Behind him, a chalkboard displays medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs.' He speaks with expressive hand gestures, emphasizing his points as he continues, 'To put it another way, in the 21st century, the real cause of contemporary tuberculosis is, for lack of a better term, us. And that's bad news. But it is also good news.' The video cuts to a montage of historical artworks and photographs, including a medieval manuscript illustration, a portrait of a woman in a green dress, and a painting of a crowded hospital ward, while the Man in Blue Suit's voice continues, explaining that 'in 1800, there was nothing anyone could do to stop people from dying of TB.' The scene returns to the Man in Blue Suit, who elaborates, 'But we no longer live in that world, thanks to the accumulation and dissemination of knowledge about the illness and how to treat it. If we are the cause of TB, we can also be the solution to TB. And that's the challenge I want to leave you with.' The final segment features a sepia-toned bar graph, visually illustrating a decline in numbers from 2.3 million in 2000 to 1.5 million in 2021, with a question mark for 2032. The Man in Blue Suit's voice aligns with the visual, stating, 'In 2000, around 2.3 million people died of tuberculosis. In 2021, 1.5 million did. In 2032, that number could decline by...' before the audio fades out.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a sepia-toned bar graph depicting a decline from 2.3 million in 2000 to 1.5 million in 2021, with a question mark for 2032, as a passionate male voice emphasizes saving millions of lives. The Man in Blue Suit, identified as John Green, stands at a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom setting, specifically the Indiana Medical History Museum, with a chalkboard displaying 1909 medical notes including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs.' He directly addresses the camera, gesturing expressively to state that "millions of lives can be saved" through increased government investment in research and "curative therapy," citing expanded access to HIV treatment and a precipitous drop in malaria deaths as past successes. Brief intercuts show a young Black man in an orange 'Partners In Health' t-shirt with medical attire outdoors and a woman walking in a hallway. Returning to John Green, he animatedly discusses the complexity and curability of TB, asserting, "We can live in a world where no one dies of tuberculosis. We are the cause, but we can also be the cure." This is followed by a split-screen effect where he continues speaking on the left, and an abstract, kaleidoscopic pattern with bold red text 'TB FIGHTERS' appears on the right, accompanied by inspiring, dramatic background music. John Green then directs viewers to the TB Fighters community to lower treatment costs, with the 'TB FIGHTERS' logo and website appearing on split-screen. As upbeat instrumental music swells, the scene transitions to a full-screen Credits Screen listing production roles and acknowledging the Indiana Medical History Museum, with John Green encouraging Patreon support for "Crash Course." Archival Source Credits from institutions like the National Library of Medicine and the Smithsonian American Art Museum are displayed, concluding with the 'COMPLEXLY' logo on a bright blue background.

Vintage Classroom Setting.description: A dimly lit, old-fashioned classroom, explicitly identified by John Green as the 'Indiana Medical History Museum,' featuring a large chalkboard displaying medical notes from 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs'. The wooden desk and visible vintage medical equipment and bottles contribute to the historical and academic atmosphere.

The scene transitions to the Man in Blue Suit, standing at a wooden lectern in a vintage classroom, with a chalkboard behind him displaying medical notes from January 26, 1909, including 'Pathology of Gross Lesions' and 'Toxic Insanities: alcohol and various drugs'. He speaks directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, emphasizing that "millions of lives can be saved" if governments invest more in research and "curative therapy." He highlights past successes, stating that "access to HIV treatment has dramatically expanded" and "malaria deaths have dropped precipitously." During his speech, the video briefly cuts to a modern-day scene showing a young Black man in an orange 'Partners In Health' t-shirt with a stethoscope, mask, and cap, standing outdoors amidst greenery, followed by a quick shot of a woman walking through a hallway. Returning to the Man in Blue Suit, he continues his animated lecture, asserting that while "treating TB is complex" and "has always been a strange disease," it "is curable." A close-up of his face emphasizes his intense delivery as he declares, "We can live in a world where no one dies of tuberculosis. We are the cause, but we can also be the cure." The scene briefly fades to black, then returns to the Man in Blue Suit, now in a split-screen effect: he continues speaking on the left, while a dark, abstract, kaleidoscopic pattern with bold red text 'TB FIGHTERS' appears on the right. Throughout this, an inspiring, dramatic background music plays. As the minute concludes, a different male voice takes over, thanking viewers for watching their "very first crash course lecture series" and inviting suggestions for future topics, with the music continuing.

The segment opens with John Green, the Man in Blue Suit, speaking directly to the camera with an engaging, expressive male voice and animated hand gestures, wearing a blue suit, white shirt, and blue-rimmed glasses against a dark, textured background.
